map.condition: Finding variables live at entry/exit of a block
Checking if variable x is live at statement S_i
map.use: \exists S_j : x \in \text{USE}(S_j),\ \text{path}(S_i \to S_j),\ \nexists \text{def}(x) \text{ between } S_i, S_j

map.condition: Counting variables needed after SSA conversion
Merging SSA definitions at a join point

formulas.name: Definition of a Live Variable
formulas.latex: x \text{ is live at } S_i \iff \exists\, S_j: (1)\; x \in \text{USE}(S_j),\; (2)\; \text{path}(S_i \to S_j) \text{ exists},\; (3)\; x \notin \text{DEF on path}
formulas.plain: x is live at S_i iff there exists S_j that uses x, a path from S_i to S_j exists, and x is not redefined on that path.
formulas.whenToUse: Determining whether a variable still holds a useful value at any program point.

terms.means: statement where liveness is checked
a later statement that uses x
set of variables read by statement S_j
set of variables defined/overwritten on the path

formulas.trap: ALL three conditions must hold simultaneously; a single counterexample (e.g., x is redefined before S_j) makes x dead.

formulas.name: USE and DEF Sets of a Basic Block
formulas.latex: \text{USE}[B] = \text{variables read before any def in } B;\quad \text{DEF}[B] = \text{variables defined in } B
formulas.plain: USE[B] = variables used in B before being defined in B; DEF[B] = variables defined (written) anywhere in B.
formulas.whenToUse: Building the per-block USE/DEF sets needed to run liveness equations.

terms.means: upward-exposed uses in block B
definitions in block B

formulas.trap: A variable that is defined then used in B belongs to DEF[B] but NOT to USE[B]; order of statements inside B matters.

groups.groupTitle: Live Variable Analysis — Data Flow Equations

formulas.name: IN[B] — Liveness at Block Entry
formulas.whenToUse: Computing which variables are live as control enters block B.

terms.means: live variables at entry of B
variables used in B before any def
live variables at exit of B
variables defined in B

formulas.trap: Subtraction (OUT minus DEF) must be computed before the union; do not add variables from OUT that are killed in B.

formulas.name: OUT[B] — Liveness at Block Exit
formulas.plain: OUT[B] = union of IN[S] for all successor blocks S of B
formulas.whenToUse: A variable is live at B's exit if it is live at entry of any successor.

terms.means: live variables at exit of B
set of immediate successors of B in the CFG
live-entry set of successor S

formulas.trap: Liveness is a BACKWARD analysis; iterate from exit blocks backward to entry, not forward.

formulas.name: Boundary Condition for Liveness
formulas.plain: OUT[exit block] = empty set (no variable is live after the program exits).
formulas.whenToUse: Initializing the iterative data-flow algorithm for liveness.

terms.means: the exit (last) block of the control flow graph

formulas.trap: If there is no explicit exit block, treat every return statement's block as an exit block with OUT = {}.

formulas.name: Iterative Liveness Algorithm
formulas.plain: Repeat (compute OUT then IN for every block in reverse postorder) until no set changes.
formulas.whenToUse: Running the backward iterative fixed-point algorithm to compute liveness for all blocks.

terms.means: no IN or OUT set changed in the last iteration

formulas.trap: Always process blocks in REVERSE postorder (exit-to-entry) for faster convergence; random order is correct but slow.

formulas.name: Dead Assignment Elimination
formulas.latex: x = e \text{ is dead (eliminable) if } x \notin \text{OUT}[S]
formulas.plain: An assignment x = e can be removed if x is not in OUT[S], i.e., x is not live after the statement.
formulas.whenToUse: Applying liveness results to eliminate dead assignments in optimization.

terms.means: set of variables live immediately after statement S

formulas.trap: Even if x is dead, the expression e may have side effects; in practice only pure assignments are removed.

formulas.name: Liveness and Register Allocation
formulas.latex: \text{Two vars } x, y \text{ interfere if } x \in \text{OUT}[S] \text{ and } y \in \text{OUT}[S] \text{ simultaneously}
formulas.plain: Variables that are live at the same time interfere and cannot share a register.
formulas.whenToUse: Building the interference graph for graph-coloring register allocation.

terms.means: set of live variables after statement S

formulas.trap: Variables live at program start (entry) do not interfere with variables live only at program end.

formulas.name: SSA Definition
formulas.latex: \text{In SSA form, every variable is defined (assigned) exactly once in the program text}
formulas.plain: Each variable has exactly one static definition site; every redefinition creates a new versioned name.
formulas.whenToUse: Understanding the fundamental invariant that SSA enforces.

terms.means: a write/assignment to a variable
textual; the same definition may execute multiple times at runtime

formulas.trap: SSA is a STATIC (textual) property, not a dynamic one; a definition inside a loop still counts as one definition.

formulas.name: Variable Versioning (Renaming)
formulas.latex: x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k \text{ are created for variable } x \text{ with } k \text{ definition sites}
formulas.plain: For each original variable x with k definitions, create k subscripted versions x_1, x_2, ..., x_k.
formulas.whenToUse: Counting how many total SSA variables are needed, or converting code to SSA.

terms.means: number of distinct definition sites for variable x
the i-th version of variable x in SSA

formulas.trap: The FIRST definition of a variable in the original code also gets a subscript (e.g., x_1), so even a single-definition variable gets renamed.

formulas.name: Total SSA Variable Count
formulas.latex: \text{SSA variables} = \sum_{x} (\text{number of definitions of } x \text{ in the original code})
formulas.plain: Total SSA variables = sum over each original variable of its definition count (including phi-function outputs).
formulas.whenToUse: Answering GATE questions that ask for the minimum number of variables in SSA form.

terms.means: each assignment statement that writes to the variable

formulas.trap: Phi-function outputs count as additional definitions; also count the variables introduced by phi-functions at join points.

formulas.name: Phi-Function Definition
formulas.plain: x_k = phi(x_i, x_j): selects x_i or x_j depending on which predecessor basic block was taken at runtime.
formulas.whenToUse: Any time two different SSA versions of the same variable merge at a control-flow join point.

terms.means: SSA version coming from one predecessor block
SSA version coming from the other predecessor block
new SSA version produced at the join point

formulas.trap: Phi-functions are only placed at join points (where multiple control paths meet); do not add them on straight-line code.

formulas.name: Phi-Placement Rule
formulas.latex: \phi\text{-function for } x \text{ at block } B \iff B \text{ is in } \text{DF}(D) \text{ for some definition block } D \text{ of } x
formulas.plain: Place a phi-function for x at block B if B is in the dominance frontier of any block that defines x.
formulas.whenToUse: Determining exactly which join points need phi-functions for a given variable.

terms.means: dominance frontier of block D
a block containing a definition of x

formulas.trap: Phi-functions may themselves introduce new definitions, requiring further phi-functions (iterated dominance frontier).

formulas.name: SSA Conversion: Counting Renamed Variables
formulas.latex: p = a-b;\; q=p*c;\; p=u*v;\; q=p+q \;\Rightarrow\; p_1,q_1,p_2,q_2 \Rightarrow 4 \text{ vars (2 per original)}
formulas.plain: Original p and q each have 2 definitions, so SSA produces p1, p2 for p and q1, q2 for q = 4 total SSA variables.
formulas.whenToUse: Template for GATE questions asking the minimum number of SSA variables for a given code segment.

terms.means: two SSA versions of p
two SSA versions of q

formulas.trap: Do NOT count read-only variables (a, b, c, u, v) if they are never redefined; they stay as a single SSA version.

formulas.name: Immediate Use-Def Chains in SSA
formulas.latex: \text{In SSA, every use of } x_i \text{ has exactly one reaching definition: the unique assignment to } x_i
formulas.plain: Each use of an SSA variable has exactly one definition, so use-def chains are trivially O(1) to compute.
formulas.whenToUse: Explaining why SSA simplifies many optimizations (constant propagation, dead-code elimination).

terms.means: the definition that may supply the value at a use point

formulas.trap: Phi-functions create multiple reaching definitions for one name in the original code; SSA resolves this by versioning.

formulas.name: CSE / Value Numbering in SSA
formulas.latex: a + b*c + b*c + a \Rightarrow t_1=b*c,\; t_2=t_1+a,\; t_3=t_2+t_1 \quad (\text{2 temps instead of 4})
formulas.plain: Common subexpressions like b*c and a need only one SSA temp each; value numbering in SSA detects them directly.
formulas.whenToUse: GATE questions asking for the minimum temporaries in SSA form for an arithmetic expression.

terms.means: SSA temporary variable for a subexpression
Common Subexpression Elimination

formulas.trap: Count distinct VALUE computations, not distinct syntactic occurrences; b*c appears twice but is ONE value in SSA.

formulas.name: Leaving SSA (Deconstruction)
formulas.latex: x_k = \phi(x_i, x_j) \Rightarrow \text{insert copy: } x_k = x_i \text{ (or } x_j\text{) in corresponding predecessor}
formulas.plain: To remove SSA, replace each phi-function with copy instructions inserted at the end of the corresponding predecessor blocks.
formulas.whenToUse: Code generation phase where SSA must be lowered to machine instructions that do not support phi-functions.

terms.means: a simple assignment used to implement the phi-function selection

formulas.trap: The lost-copy and swap problems can arise when two phi-functions interfere; use the correct sequencing of copies.
